statement: vLLM exists because traditional LLM inference pre-allocates contiguous KV-cache memory for the maximum sequence length, wasting 60-80% of GPU memory on internal and external fragmentation. PagedAttention solves this by managing KV cache in fixed-size non-contiguous 'pages' (blocks), similar to OS virtual memory paging. This lets multiple requests share physical memory pages for common prefix tokens and eliminates pre-allocation waste, achieving 24x higher throughput than HuggingFace Transformers at equivalent latency targets.

vLLM is designed as a drop-in OpenAI API replacement: running `python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server --model <model>` starts a server that accepts the same /v1/completions, /v1/chat/completions, and /v1/embeddings endpoints as OpenAI's API. Clients written for OpenAI (LangChain, LlamaIndex, any openai-python SDK user) need only change the base_url and api_key — zero code changes required. This is intentional product strategy: lower adoption friction for teams migrating from OpenAI to self-hosted inference.

vLLM defaults gpu_memory_utilization=0.9, meaning it pre-reserves 90% of GPU VRAM for the KV-cache page pool at startup. Under concurrent high-context requests this headroom is consumed entirely, producing CUDA out-of-memory errors that crash the worker process — not a graceful 429. The fix is NOT to lower gpu_memory_utilization blindly: lowering it reduces throughput. The correct approach is to (1) profile actual peak KV-cache usage with `--max-num-seqs` and `--max-model-len` constraints, (2) set gpu_memory_utilization to leave headroom for CUDA kernels (~5-10%), and (3) configure `--max-num-batched-tokens` to cap total in-flight token count. Many teams hit this on their first multi-user deployment because single-user benchmarks never exhaust the pool.

vLLM treats AWQ, GPTQ, FP8, and GGUF as distinct quantization backends with separate kernel implementations — they are NOT interchangeable. Common failure modes: (1) Loading an AWQ-quantized model without `--quantization awq` causes silent weight corruption or crashes, not a clear error. (2) GPTQ models require `--quantization gptq` and are slower than AWQ on vLLM because vLLM's GPTQ path uses Marlin kernels only on Ampere+, falling back to slow triton on older GPUs. (3) FP8 is only supported on Hopper (H100/H200) and Ada Lovelace (L40S); using `--quantization fp8` on A100 raises an unsupported hardware error. (4) GGUF is not natively supported in the main vLLM server — it requires llama.cpp or conversion first. Always verify the quantization format matches both the `--quantization` flag AND the target GPU architecture before deployment.

vLLM tensor parallelism (--tensor-parallel-size N) requires all N GPUs to have identical VRAM capacity and ideally be connected via NVLink or NVSwitch. Critical gotchas: (1) Mixed GPU types in one tensor-parallel group (e.g., A100-40GB + A100-80GB) will crash because vLLM assumes symmetric sharding — the smaller GPU OOMs during weight load. (2) PCIe-connected GPUs (no NVLink) suffer severe all-reduce latency; for models that fit on a single GPU, single-GPU is faster. (3) `--tensor-parallel-size` must evenly divide the model's attention head count — using TP=3 on a model with 32 heads will raise a configuration error at startup. (4) Pipeline parallelism (--pipeline-parallel-size) is orthogonal to tensor parallelism but multiplies coordination overhead; prefer TP over PP for latency-sensitive workloads.

vLLM uses continuous batching (also called iteration-level scheduling), where new requests are inserted into the active batch the moment a slot frees — no waiting for the entire batch to complete. This is architecturally opposite to static batching (used in naive servers), which holds the GPU idle until every request in a batch finishes its slowest sequence. For mixed-length workloads (typical chat: some 50-token replies, some 2000-token replies), continuous batching delivers 3-10x higher GPU utilization. The implication for operators: latency SLOs should be set per-request (time-to-first-token + inter-token latency), NOT as batch throughput, because individual request latency is now the meaningful unit.

Many models on HuggingFace Hub require `--trust-remote-code` to load custom tokenizers, attention implementations, or architecture files that are not part of vLLM's built-in model registry. Enabling this flag executes arbitrary Python code downloaded from the Hub at model load time — it is a supply-chain attack surface. The security contract is: trust is transitive from the model card to all files in the repo at the pinned revision. Safe practices: (1) Always pin a specific commit SHA (`--revision <sha>`), not a branch or tag, when using trust-remote-code in production. (2) Audit the modeling_*.py files in the repo before deploying. (3) Air-gapped deployments should mirror the model to a private registry and verify checksums. Never use `--trust-remote-code` with `--revision main` in production — main can be updated silently.

vLLM's CPU backend (`--device cpu`) is explicitly experimental and unsuitable for production inference. Key limitations: (1) No PagedAttention on CPU — memory management falls back to eager allocation, so the primary vLLM value proposition disappears. (2) Throughput is 10-50x lower than GPU depending on model size; even a single A10G outperforms a 64-core CPU server for token generation. (3) Quantization support on CPU is limited to INT8 via PyTorch; AWQ and FP8 are GPU-only. (4) Continuous batching scheduler still runs but KV-cache capacity is constrained by system RAM, and swapping between RAM and disk is not implemented in the CPU path. Use the CPU backend only for functional correctness testing or on-device edge deployments where latency requirements are loose (>5s acceptable). For cost-sensitive CPU-only inference, llama.cpp or Ollama are better-architected alternatives.

vLLM's prefix caching feature (enable via --enable-prefix-caching) reuses KV cache pages for identical prompt prefixes across requests. When multiple requests share a long system prompt or RAG preamble, the prefix pages are computed once and then reused from cache for subsequent requests — effectively free tokens for all but the first requester in a burst. The hit rate is highest for: (1) chat applications with a fixed system prompt, (2) RAG systems where the same retrieved chunks appear across queries, (3) batch classification tasks with a fixed instruction prefix. The cache is keyed on the exact byte sequence of the prefix, so even minor prompt variations (different whitespace, reordered messages) produce cache misses. Prefix caching is complementary to continuous batching and has zero overhead for cache misses — the only cost is the block metadata bookkeeping, which is negligible.

vLLM's speculative decoding (--speculative-model) requires that the draft model and target model share identical vocabulary and tokenizer. Using a mismatched draft model (e.g., a 1B distilled model with a different tokenizer than the 70B target) causes silent token ID misalignment: the draft produces tokens that are accepted by the verifier's numerical check but decode to wrong text in the target's vocabulary. This manifests as subtly garbled output rather than an error. Always verify tokenizer identity with `AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(draft).vocab_size == AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(target).vocab_size` and ideally confirm they share the same tokenizer class before enabling speculative decoding in production.

vLLM exposes Prometheus metrics at /metrics when --enable-prometheus is set. The four metrics that matter most for SLO monitoring are: vllm:time_to_first_token_seconds (TTFT histogram), vllm:time_per_output_token_seconds (TPOT histogram for inter-token latency), vllm:num_requests_running (active requests in flight), and vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc (KV cache fill %). The cache usage metric is the leading indicator for latency degradation: when it exceeds 90%, the scheduler starts preempting lower-priority requests to free pages, causing TTFT spikes on newly arriving requests. Setting an alert on gpu_cache_usage_perc > 0.85 gives a 5-10 minute warning before latency SLOs are violated under typical load growth patterns.

vLLM's --max-model-len parameter truncates input prompts silently by default when they exceed the configured limit. A request with a 16K-token prompt sent to a server configured with --max-model-len 8192 will be truncated to 8192 tokens from the right (the end), not rejected. The model then generates a continuation of a truncated context, often producing incoherent output with no error returned to the client. Production deployments should set --max-model-len to match the model's true context window, and application code should validate prompt length before sending. Alternatively, configuring truncation_on_max_len=True in the request raises an explicit error instead of silently truncating.

vLLM's LoRA serving (--enable-lora, --max-loras N) allows multiple LoRA adapters to be loaded simultaneously and switched per-request using the model field (e.g., 'base-model/lora-adapter-name'). The key architectural detail is that base model weights are shared across all LoRA requests; only the adapter tensors are swapped. This makes LoRA serving far more memory-efficient than running separate model instances per adapter. The constraint is --max-loras limits how many adapters reside in GPU memory simultaneously — excess adapters are evicted to CPU and reloaded on demand, incurring a latency spike on the first request to an evicted adapter. For latency-sensitive multi-tenant deployments, set --max-loras to the number of frequently used adapters and accept CPU fallback only for rarely-used ones.

vLLM's structured output generation (--guided-decoding-backend outlines|lm-format-enforcer) constrains token sampling to match a JSON schema or regex. The critical performance constraint: guided decoding requires building a finite state machine (FSM) from the schema on each unique request schema, which takes 50-500ms for complex schemas. This FSM construction cost is paid once per unique schema and then cached, but the first request for each schema adds latency. In high-QPS services where each user submits a different schema, the FSM cache will thrash and add consistent overhead. The mitigation is to normalize schemas at the application layer (deduplicate, canonicalize field ordering) before passing to vLLM, maximizing FSM cache hits. Outlines backend generally outperforms lm-format-enforcer for complex nested schemas.

vLLM's chunked prefill (--enable-chunked-prefill) splits large prompt prefills across multiple scheduler iterations instead of processing them in a single blocking step. Without chunked prefill, a single 32K-token prompt monopolizes the GPU for the entire prefill duration (often 5-15 seconds on large models), causing all other in-flight requests to stall — a 'head-of-line blocking' problem. Chunked prefill breaks this monopoly by interleaving prefill chunks with decode steps for other requests, dramatically improving P99 TTFT for shorter concurrent requests. The tradeoff is that the long prompt's own TTFT increases slightly because its prefill is spread across multiple steps. For mixed-workload deployments (some long-context, many short-context requests), chunked prefill is almost always a net win at the system level.

vLLM multi-node inference using Ray (--worker-use-ray with multiple hosts) has a common deployment failure where workers start successfully but inference silently hangs. The root cause is almost always network asymmetry: Ray's collective communication (NCCL all-reduce for tensor parallelism across nodes) requires symmetric bidirectional bandwidth between all worker pairs, but production Kubernetes or cloud networks often have asymmetric routing or firewall rules that block NCCL's preferred port range (starting at 29400). Diagnosing this requires running `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` to see which NCCL operation stalls. Fixes: (1) open NCCL port range in security groups, (2) set NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME to the correct network interface explicitly, (3) use InfiniBand over RoCE when available. Silent hangs rather than error messages make this failure class disproportionately time-consuming to diagnose.
